publication.title: Hard wiring of T cell receptor specificity for the major histocompatibility complex is underpinned by TCR adaptability.
publication.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
publication.authors: Burrows SR, Chen Z, Archbold JK, Tynan FE, Beddoe T, Kjer-Nielsen L, Miles JJ, Khanna R, Moss DJ, Liu YC, Gras S, Kostenko L, Brennan RM, Clements CS, Brooks AG, Purcell AW, McCluskey J, Rossjohn J.

executive_summary: This study examines αβ TCR recognition of pMHC-I complexes across multiple TCR–HLA-B*0801 and HLA-B*3508 interactions using structural analysis and mutagenesis of three conserved MHC-I contact residues (positions 65, 69, and 155). Although these three positions are contacted by TCRs across diverse complexes in a conserved docking geometry, individual or combined mutations at these sites did not uniformly abolish TCR recognition, and impaired binding could be partially restored by CD8 coreceptor engagement. Crystal structures of TCR-pMHC-I complexes bearing all three mutations revealed that TCRs compensate through shifted footprints and formation of alternative interactions, demonstrating unexpected adaptability despite the apparent constraint of MHC-restricted specificity. This work challenges the prevailing model of rigid TCR-MHC docking and shows that TCR specificity is underpinned by structural flexibility rather than invariant molecular contacts, a finding relevant to understanding how TCRs maintain functional recognition across diverse MHC variants and may inform TCR engineering for cross-HLA recognition in immunotherapy.
